explanation.text: The sentence places the story in a time when farmers used oxen and sleds and relied on lanterns to light the darkness. Those outdated tools show that the events happened long ago.
The other children make snowballs and "pelted" them at crows while Willie studies flakes. Because snowballs are objects the children send toward the birds, the word means "threw."

Willie's central goal is to preserve the beauty he sees in snowflakes so other people can experience it too. His declaration that everyone can now see a tiny crystal's beauty shows that his successful photograph fulfills that goal.

Paragraph 9 details how Willie waits for hours in the cold, keeps the shed unheated, avoids breathing or twitching, and works before each flake melts. This careful account of his persistence reveals the author's admiration for his dedication.

Willie's camera cost his parents their savings and as much as ten cows, showing that his work required costly equipment. Later, he never becomes rich because he spends every penny on his pictures, confirming his willingness to devote all his money to the work.

Willie gives photographs away, sells them for only a few cents, creates birthday gifts, and holds slide shows for children and adults. These choices show that sharing his discoveries mattered more to him than earning money.

The article follows Willie from his childhood fascination with snow through years of experiments, his first successful photograph, and his later recognition as an expert. This progression shows that his life and study of snow are presented chronologically.

At the beginning, Gregor and his mother work hard but remain too poor to buy the flock they want. By paragraph 23, he has sold gems, bought a huge flock, and shared a fortune, so the two paragraphs show how completely his life changes.

MacTavish turns and "marched back" immediately after Gregor refuses to sell, then announces that he will pay anything. His forceful return and increased offer show that he is determined to obtain the sheep.

The market seller falsely claims the sickly sheep will make its owner rich so Gregor will buy it. Gregor later spreads the same claim to attract MacTavish, which shows that both men use a trick in hopes of gaining wealth.

After gaining MacTavish's fortune, Gregor shares the remaining wealth with poor families who were cheated. This generous action shows how he differs from MacTavish, who repeatedly keeps money earned by other farmers.

Gregor uses his new wealth to help his mother and the other struggling families in the valley. MacTavish, by contrast, cheats those same neighbors and gives them less than they earn, demonstrating that Gregor is generous while MacTavish is not.

MacTavish already has houses, gems, and costly clothing, yet his desire for even more wealth makes him trade an entire mansion for one sheep. His enormous loss demonstrates the story's warning that greed can carry a high price.

The rumor about a wealth-producing sheep draws greedy MacTavish to Gregor, where he agrees to exchange his mansion and possessions for the animal. This event belongs in a summary because it drives the trick that changes both men's fortunes.

The mugumo provides fruit for Wangari, geckos, and elephants, nesting places for birds, branches for monkeys, and sacred shade for Kikuyu ancestors. These details show that the tree matters to both the community and many animals.

A seed in rich soil has the conditions to keep developing, just as Wangari's educated mind is prepared to develop further. The comparison therefore shows her desire and readiness to continue learning.

The government first sells land to companies that remove native forests. The passage then traces the results—lost habitats, scarce firewood, eroded soil, muddy rivers, failed crops, and hunger—so the details move from a cause to its effects.

Wangari calls on other mothers to work with her, then travels among villages, towns, and cities carrying seeds, saplings, and tools. The women plant trees at each stop, which shows that she spreads the idea by sharing it with women around the country.

Wangari urges the women to work together, and their planting grows from one tree to thirty million despite the problem's enormous scale. Their success supports the view that determined cooperation enables people to create the change they seek.

The title's "seeds of change" are both the actual seeds Wangari organizes women to plant and the beginning of a much larger movement. It supports the main idea by showing how her tree-planting plan solves the environmental problems harming Kenyan families.

As a girl, Wangari's mother teaches her that the mugumo shelters and feeds many living things and is sacred to their people. Wangari's promise never to cut it down shows how that early lesson could inspire her later plan to restore Kenya by planting trees.
